challenges.title: All Active Customers
challenges.description: Return the first_name and last_name of every customer whose status is 'active'. Order by last_name ascending.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT, status TEXT, city TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM customers WHERE status = 'active' ORDER BY last_name ASC;
hints: Use a WHERE clause to filter rows by the status column.
Remember that text values must be wrapped in single quotes: status = 'active'.
Add ORDER BY last_name ASC at the end to sort the results.

challenges.title: Count Customers by City
challenges.description: Return each city and the number of customers in it, as columns (city, customer_count). Order by customer_count descending.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT, status TEXT, city TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS customer_count FROM customers GROUP BY city ORDER BY customer_count DESC;
hints: You need GROUP BY to collapse rows that share the same city.
COUNT(*) counts the rows in each group.
Alias the count with AS customer_count so the column name matches.

challenges.title: Unique Cities
challenges.description: Return a single column 'city' listing each distinct city exactly once. Order alphabetically.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT, status TEXT, city TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT DISTINCT city FROM customers ORDER BY city ASC;
hints: DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from the result.
Select only the city column so duplicates can be collapsed.
Add ORDER BY city to sort alphabetically.

challenges.title: Total Revenue
challenges.description: Return a single value in a column named total_revenue representing the sum of all order amounts.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL, order_date TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT SUM(amount) AS total_revenue FROM orders;
hints: SUM() adds up all values in a numeric column.
You don't need GROUP BY when summing the whole table.
Alias the result AS total_revenue.

challenges.title: High-Value Orders
challenges.description: Return the id and amount of all orders with an amount greater than 100. Order by amount descending.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL, order_date TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT id, amount FROM orders WHERE amount > 100 ORDER BY amount DESC;
hints: Use WHERE amount > 100 to keep only large orders.
Greater-than is the > operator (strictly greater, so 100 is excluded).
ORDER BY amount DESC sorts highest first.

challenges.title: Names Starting With A
challenges.description: Return first_name and last_name of customers whose first_name starts with the letter 'A'.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT, status TEXT, city TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM customers WHERE first_name LIKE 'A%';
hints: LIKE lets you match text patterns.
The % wildcard matches any sequence of characters.
'A%' means 'starts with A, followed by anything'.

challenges.title: Three Most Recent Orders
challenges.description: Return the id and order_date of the 3 most recent orders (latest order_date first).
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL, order_date TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT id, order_date FROM orders ORDER BY order_date DESC LIMIT 3;
hints: Sort by order_date descending to put the newest first.
LIMIT 3 keeps only the top 3 rows after sorting.
Order matters: ORDER BY comes before LIMIT.

challenges.title: Orders With Customer Names
challenges.description: Return each order's id, the customer's first_name, and the order amount, joining orders to customers. Order by order id.
challenges.schema:
CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT o.id, c.first_name, o.amount FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id ORDER BY o.id;
hints: Use JOIN to combine rows from orders and customers.
The join condition links orders.customer_id to customers.id.
Use table aliases (o, c) to keep column references clear.

challenges.title: Customers With No Orders
challenges.description: Return the first_name and last_name of customers who have never placed an order.
challenges.schema:
CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT c.first_name, c.last_name FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.id IS NULL;
hints: A LEFT JOIN keeps all customers, even those with no matching orders.
Customers with no orders will have NULL in the orders columns.
Filter with WHERE o.id IS NULL to keep only the unmatched customers.

challenges.title: Customers With More Than 2 Orders
challenges.description: Return the customer_id and order count (as order_count) for customers who placed more than 2 orders.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;
hints: GROUP BY customer_id groups each customer's orders together.
HAVING filters groups after aggregation (WHERE can't use COUNT).
Use HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 to keep customers with more than two orders.

challenges.title: Total Spent Per Customer
challenges.description: Return first_name and total_spent (sum of order amounts) for each customer who has at least one order. Order by total_spent descending.
challenges.schema:
CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT c.first_name, SUM(o.amount) AS total_spent FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id GROUP BY c.id ORDER BY total_spent DESC;
hints: Join customers to orders, then GROUP BY the customer.
SUM(o.amount) totals each customer's order amounts.
An INNER JOIN naturally excludes customers with no orders.

challenges.title: Above-Average Orders
challenges.description: Return the id and amount of orders whose amount is greater than the average order amount.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT id, amount FROM orders WHERE amount > (SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders);
hints: First compute the average: SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders.
Use that as a subquery inside a WHERE clause.
WHERE amount > (subquery) keeps only above-average orders.

challenges.title: Label Order Sizes
challenges.description: Return each order's id, amount, and a column 'size' that is 'large' when amount >= 100, otherwise 'small'.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT id, amount, CASE WHEN amount >= 100 THEN 'large' ELSE 'small' END AS size FROM orders;
hints: CASE WHEN ... THEN ... ELSE ... END creates conditional values.
The condition is amount >= 100.
Alias the whole CASE expression AS size.

challenges.title: Orders in January 2024
challenges.description: Return the id and order_date of all orders placed in January 2024.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL, order_date TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT id, order_date FROM orders WHERE strftime('%Y-%m', order_date) = '2024-01';
hints: SQLite stores these dates as text in YYYY-MM-DD format.
strftime('%Y-%m', order_date) extracts the year-month.
Compare that to '2024-01' to isolate January 2024.

challenges.title: Most Recent Order Per Customer
challenges.description: For each customer, return the single most recent order (customer_id, id, order_date). Use a window function.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL, order_date TEXT);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT customer_id, id, order_date FROM (SELECT customer_id, id, order_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn FROM orders) WHERE rn = 1;
hints: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) ranks each customer's orders.
The most recent order gets rn = 1 within each customer partition.
Wrap it in a subquery and filter WHERE rn = 1.

challenges.title: Rank Orders by Amount
challenges.description: Return id, amount, and a column 'amount_rank' ranking all orders by amount descending (highest = rank 1). Use RANK().
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT id, amount, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY amount DESC) AS amount_rank FROM orders;
hints: RANK() OVER (ORDER BY amount DESC) assigns ranks across all rows.
Equal amounts receive the same rank (that's how RANK differs from ROW_NUMBER).
Alias the window expression AS amount_rank.

challenges.title: Customers Above Average Spend (CTE)
challenges.description: Using a CTE, return first_name and total_spent for customers whose total spend exceeds the average total spend across all customers.
challenges.schema:
CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, first_name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: WITH spend AS (SELECT c.id, c.first_name, SUM(o.amount) AS total_spent FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id GROUP BY c.id) SELECT first_name, total_spent FROM spend WHERE total_spent > (SELECT AVG(total_spent) FROM spend);
hints: Define a CTE with WITH spend AS (...) that totals each customer's spend.
Then query the CTE and compare each total to the average of the CTE.
You can reference the CTE more than once, including inside a subquery.

challenges.title: Running Total of Orders
challenges.description: Return id, amount, and a running_total column that accumulates amount ordered by id ascending. Use a window function.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT id, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY id) AS running_total FROM orders;
hints: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY id) creates a cumulative sum.
The ORDER BY inside OVER defines the accumulation order.
No GROUP BY needed — the window keeps every row.

challenges.title: Employees and Their Managers
challenges.description: Return each employee's name and their manager's name (as employee_name, manager_name). Employees with no manager should be excluded.
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, manager_id INTEGER);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT e.name AS employee_name, m.name AS manager_name FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
hints: Join the employees table to itself using two aliases (e and m).
Link e.manager_id to m.id to find each employee's manager.
An INNER JOIN automatically drops employees whose manager_id is NULL.

challenges.title: Order Amount Change
challenges.description: For orders ordered by id, return id, amount, and prev_amount (the amount of the previous order, NULL for the first). Use LAG().
challenges.schema: CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
challenges.correct_answer: SELECT id, amount, LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY id) AS prev_amount FROM orders;
hints: LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY id) fetches the previous row's amount.
The first row has no previous row, so it returns NULL.
Alias the result AS prev_amount.
